text: Hello, I'm sharing another laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
This one being done for biliary dyskinesia.
The video shows a large, distended gallbladder with a normal appearance, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon aspirates bile from the distended gallbladder to reduce its tension, facilitating easier manipulation with graspers and minimizing the risk of tearing.
The surgeon prepares to begin the normal dissection of the gallbladder following the aspiration of bile to reduce its distension.
The surgeon begins dissection in the fatty peritoneum overlying the bottom of the gallbladder, preparing to manipulate the gallbladder during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon begins lateral dissection to the patient's right side, focusing on the area surrounding the gallbladder.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum to facilitate manipulation of the distended gallbladder, improving the angles for better visualization during the procedure.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum to expose the wall of the gallbladder, facilitating better visualization and manipulation during the procedure.
The surgeon deepens the dissection behind the gallbladder, carefully separating tissue layers to improve access and visibility.
This helps in achieving the critical view of safety.
The surgeon performs dissection on the medial or left side of the gallbladder, mirroring the lateral dissection previously completed on the patient's right side.
We need to acknowledge that the artery is going to be in this vicinity.
And we don't want to inadvertently injure that, so I always make sure that I can see through what I'm cauterizing.
The surgeon identifies a node near the gallbladder, indicating the proximity of the artery. The dissection is positioned laterally and medially relative to the node.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the medial wall of the gallbladder, mirroring the technique used on the opposite side to achieve the critical view of safety.
The surgeon begins dissection to locate critical structures near the gallbladder, carefully navigating the surgical field to identify anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon identifies a lymph node, indicating the proximity of the artery, and carefully examines the area to avoid injury during dissection.
The surgeon clears fatty tissue on either side of the node, ensuring visibility of the artery nearby.
The surgeon visually identifies the artery adjacent to the node, confirming its location during the dissection process.
The surgeon works through the fatty tissue overlying the Calot's triangle, which appears smaller due to the large size of the gallbladder.
The surgeon continues dissection in the vicinity of the previously identified artery, aiming to locate the cystic duct amidst the surrounding fatty tissue.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to carefully cauterize tissue, ensuring visibility through the cauterized areas.
The surgeon attempts to swing the bottom of the gallbladder away from the liver, manipulating the gallbladder to create separation from the liver tissue.
The surgeon begins to identify the cystic duct, working carefully in the area where the duct is expected to be located.
The surgeon opens up the fatty tissue overlying the Calot's triangle, aiming to visualize the liver edge through the triangle.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to perform blunt dissection, attempting to open up the space around the duct for better visibility and access.
The surgeon ensures the cystic duct is clearly visible before proceeding with clipping and dividing it.
The surgeon uses a hook to meticulously clear fatty tissue surrounding the duct, ensuring no posterior arteries or accessory ducts are present.
The surgeon visually identifies the cystic duct and cystic artery, clearly exposed within the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the cystic duct and artery after achieving a confident critical view, despite minimal dissection of the gallbladder's back wall from the liver.
The surgeon proceeds to remove the gallbladder from the liver bed, detaching it from the surrounding tissue.
These cases of dyskinesia are often a pleasure because it's usually a normal appearing gallbladder.
This one was a little bit larger than normal, but still relatively easy comparatively.
The surgeon searches for the plane between the adventitial tissues and the gallbladder wall, aiming to separate these layers during the dissection.
Obviously you don't want to make a hole in the gallbladder and you don't want to get into any liver bleeding.
It's important to have good visualization and tension and counter tension to allow for the cautery to work well.
The surgeon continues to dissect upwards along the gallbladder, maintaining careful separation between the adventitial tissues and the gallbladder wall.
And you should have one of the ports open to vent when I do this because it can get very smoky inside the abdomen.
The surgeon removes the remaining attachments of the gallbladder from the surrounding adventitial tissues, completing the dissection.
The surgeon uses a twelve millimeter trocar placed in the subxiphoid region to introduce a bag for extracting the gallbladder.
Patient did very well.
I hope you enjoyed.
